title: Interactive Rebase, Autosquash, and Safe Force-Push
description:
## Cleaning up a shared feature branch

You pushed a feature branch and got review feedback. You make a follow-up commit that fixes commit `abc123`, then want to fold it into `abc123` and republish a tidy history. You run:

```bash
git commit --fixup=abc123
git rebase -i --autosquash origin/main
# ... then publish the rewritten branch
```

Teammates may have fetched your branch in the meantime. Which statement is correct?

options.text: `--fixup=abc123` plus `git rebase -i --autosquash` auto-reorders the fixup commit directly beneath `abc123` and marks it `fixup` so it is squashed without keeping its message; because rebasing rewrites commit SHAs the branch has diverged from the remote, so you must force-push, and `git push --force-with-lease` is preferred because it aborts if the remote ref advanced since your last fetch, avoiding clobbering a teammate's pushed work.

`--autosquash` only works with `--fixup` if you also pass `--no-ff`; and `git push --force` is safer than `--force-with-lease` because the latter can be tricked by a stale local tracking ref into overwriting new commits.

A fixup commit keeps its own commit message after squashing (unlike `--squash`), and force-pushing is unnecessary because rebasing onto `origin/main` preserves the original commit SHAs as long as no conflicts occur.
`--force-with-lease` and `--force` are functionally identical on a branch you own; the only difference is that `--force-with-lease` also runs `git gc` before pushing to shrink the pack.

explanation: A commit made with `git commit --fixup=<sha>` gets a subject line like `fixup! <original subject>`; `git rebase -i --autosquash` then automatically relocates it immediately after its target commit and pre-marks it `fixup`, which squashes the change in and **discards** the fixup commit's message (whereas `--squash` would keep/combine the message — this is why (c) is wrong). Rebasing recomputes commit objects, so every rewritten commit gets a new SHA and the branch diverges from its remote counterpart, requiring a force-push. `--force-with-lease` is the safe form: it only overwrites the remote ref if that ref still matches what you last observed (your remote-tracking ref), so if a teammate pushed in the meantime the push is rejected instead of silently discarding their commits — exactly the opposite of (b)'s claim. (b) also invents a `--no-ff` requirement that doesn't exist. (d) is wrong: the two are not identical and `--force-with-lease` does not run gc. The real subtlety with lease: blindly fetching/refreshing tracking refs before the push can defeat the protection, which is why you shouldn't `git fetch` immediately before a force-with-lease.

hints.title: fixup vs squash
hints.body: Both combine commits during autosquash; squash lets you keep/edit the combined message, fixup silently drops the fixup commit's message.

hints.title: Why lease beats force
hints.body: force-with-lease only pushes if the remote ref is still where you last saw it, so it refuses to overwrite commits a teammate pushed after your last fetch. Plain --force overwrites unconditionally.
